food_name: Nasi Kerabu (Kelantanese Blue Butterfly-Pea Rice with Herbs)
food_subgroup: Butterfly-pea dyed blue rice with raw herb accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Nasi Kerabu. Closest parent is rice dish with herbal accompaniments (FOODON:03315595 — rice dish). The butterfly-pea (Clitoria ternatea) flower dye has no nutritional ontology entry in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Blue-dyed rice with raw herb salad accompaniments (Kelantanese nasi kerabu style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for nasi kerabu. A00PB (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest parent. Butterfly-pea dye and raw herb accompaniments are not separately coded in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Kerabu or butterfly-pea rice dish as of 2026.

preparation_method: boiled rice dyed with butterfly-pea flower extract

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi kerabu weighs approximately 400g, comprising blue-dyed rice, a generous ring of raw herbs, fried chicken or dried fish, salted egg, grated coconut, and a small portion of budu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Kerabu — IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Nasi Kerabu, an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white jasmine rice and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Kerabu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the jasmine rice base (GI ~73 per University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward for the substantial raw herb fibre content (2.5g dietary fibre/100g), grated coconut fat, and fermented condiment components that collectively slow gastric emptying. The composite GI of 65 (medium) is a defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load of 54.6 is calculated as (65 × 21g carbohydrate × 400g serving) / 100 = 54.6g per plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from budu (fermented anchovy sauce) and salted egg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request budu to be served separately and limit intake to less than 5g per serving, as a standard 10–15g portion of budu alone contributes approximately 1,000–1,500mg sodium; replacing salted egg with hard-boiled egg further reduces sodium load, while the raw herb accompaniments are nutritious and low in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) from jasmine rice base, partially offset by high dietary fibre from raw herbs
type2_diabetes.guidance: Nasi kerabu has a medium GI of approximately 65 and a high glycemic load per plate (~54.6g); patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (200–250g), prioritise the herb accompaniments, and avoid adding extra budu or sweet condiments to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium from budu and salted egg; low saturated fat from herb-dominant composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The sodium content of a full nasi kerabu plate can exceed 1,940mg, which is a significant concern for cardiovascular patients; however, the dish's low saturated fat (1.0g/100g), high dietary fibre (2.5g/100g), and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from raw herbs (lemongrass, kaffir lime leaf, turmeric leaf, torch ginger flower) are cardioprotective — patients should minimise budu and salted egg while freely consuming the herb accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from raw herbs consumed fresh
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nasi kerabu is exceptionally rich in anti-inflammatory herbs consumed raw — torch ginger flower, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaf, and turmeric leaf all provide anti-inflammatory phytochemicals; high folate (35mcg/100g) supports joint health and the low saturated fat profile is appropriate for rheumatoid arthritis management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Kerabu is the iconic rice dish of Kelantan on Malaysia's east coast, instantly recognisable by its vivid blue colour derived from butterfly-pea flower (bunga telang) dye — a natural anthocyanin pigment with no significant caloric contribution. The rice base is jasmine rice cooked with the blue flower extract, giving it a striking indigo-blue hue that is unique among Southeast Asian rice dishes. The real nutritional story of nasi kerabu lies in its extraordinary herb accompaniments: the plate is ringed with fresh raw herbs including shaved torch ginger flower (bunga kantan), thinly sliced lemongrass, kaffir lime leaf, turmeric leaf, and long bean — creating a raw herb salad with intensely aromatic flavours and significant phytochemical content. The protein component is typically fried chicken (ayam percik or ayam goreng) or dried or fried fish. Grated coconut (kelapa parut) adds texture and mild fat. The defining condiment is budu — a pungent fermented anchovy fish sauce unique to Kelantan — which is extremely high in sodium (exceeding 10,000mg/100g) and adds deep umami character. Nutritionally, nasi kerabu provides 148 kcal per 100g with 6.5g protein, 4.2g fat, and 21g carbohydrate. It has one of the highest dietary fibre contents among Malay rice dishes (2.5g/100g) due to the volume of raw herbs. Sodium is the primary clinical concern, driven almost entirely by budu and salted egg. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a traditional Kelantanese breakfast and lunch staple, widely available at Kelantanese restaurants, Malaysian food courts, Bazaar Ramadan stalls, and during Hari Raya celebrations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research Malaysia), which is the approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2. The source is an exact match for Nasi Kerabu. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using jasmine rice as the base, adjusted for fibre content, which is the standard defensible approach for composite dishes without a direct GI measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Nasi Kerabu as a composite dish is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry; these values are not available in the approved source as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD source). All core macros (protein_g 6.5g, fat_g 4.2g, carbohydrate_g 21.0g) are non-zero and sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice GI (~73, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward for the high raw herb fibre content and fat from grated coconut; no direct GI measurement for Nasi Kerabu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Glycemic load of 54.6 calculated as (65 × 21 × 400) / 100 = 54.6 per standard 400g plate. Sodium of 485mg/100g reflects a composite dish average including budu contribution; actual sodium in a full plate may be significantly higher depending on budu quantity served. FoodEx2 code A00PB used as closest parent — no exact code for nasi kerabu exists. This is a Malaysian dish (not Singaporean), so Hindi local name is not mandatory; Hindi field set to null.
